content.text: The build-buy-partner matrix is a structured decision framework for determining how an enterprise sources each AI capability in its portfolio. Build means developing the capability internally using the firm's own engineering and data science teams. Buy means acquiring a commercial solution — a SaaS AI platform, a pre-trained model with a vendor-managed API, or a licensed agent framework. Partner means co-developing the capability with an external organization (a research institution, a technology company, or a domain expert firm) under a shared investment and IP arrangement. The decision is not made once at the program level; it is made independently for each capability in the portfolio, and the optimal choice changes as technology matures.

The build-buy-partner decision is one of the highest-leverage choices in an enterprise AI program. A firm that builds everything it needs — including commodity capabilities that vendors already provide at high quality — wastes engineering talent and delays value delivery. A firm that buys everything sacrifices competitive differentiation: if the firm's AI capabilities are available to any competitor with a credit card, they cannot constitute a strategic advantage. A firm that partners on capabilities it could build in-house gives away IP it did not need to share. Getting this decision right for each capability in the portfolio is a primary determinant of the program's return on invested capital.
Buy decisions accelerate time-to-value for commodity capabilities: a vendor's document extraction API can be deployed in weeks versus months for a custom model.
Build decisions preserve competitive differentiation: capabilities that encode the firm's proprietary knowledge, data, or domain expertise cannot be replicated by competitors who buy the same vendor.

Partner decisions enable capability development that is beyond the firm's unilateral reach: novel AI research, specialized domain datasets, or distribution access that requires an ecosystem relationship.

The framework prevents organizational defaults: without a structured decision, firms default to either 'always build' (engineering-led culture) or 'always buy' (risk-averse culture), neither of which is optimal.

Explicit decisions are revisable: a buy decision made today can be revisited in 18 months when the internal team has the capacity to build; documenting the decision and its drivers makes the revision straightforward.

Capability classifications change: a capability that required a build decision in 2022 (custom document extraction) may be a buy decision in 2025 (mature vendor offerings exist); the framework requires periodic re-evaluation.
Partner decisions carry IP and alignment risk: co-development agreements require detailed IP assignment clauses; misaligned partner incentives create friction that delays delivery.

Build decisions underestimate total cost: the full cost of building includes not just development but ongoing model maintenance, retraining, infrastructure, and security — costs that vendor solutions absorb.
Buy decisions create vendor dependency: a critical capability built on a vendor API creates a single point of failure and exposes the firm to pricing changes and service discontinuation.
The matrix does not decide vendor selection: a buy decision establishes that the firm will use a vendor; which vendor requires a separate evaluation process.

The decision framework evaluates each capability on four dimensions. First, strategic differentiation: is this capability a source of competitive advantage that is specific to this firm's data, domain expertise, or customer relationship? If yes, build is the default. Second, build feasibility: does the firm have the engineering talent, data, and infrastructure to build this capability at acceptable cost and timeline? If no, buy or partner, regardless of strategic value. Third, market maturity: does a vendor solution exist that meets the firm's quality, security, and compliance requirements? If yes, buy is strongly indicated unless strategic differentiation argues otherwise. Fourth, data sovereignty: does the use case involve data that cannot leave the firm's controlled environment? If yes, buy from a vendor that supports private deployment or build.

For agentic AI specifically, the matrix often produces a split decision: the agent framework (orchestration, tool calling, memory management) is a buy decision (use an established framework like LangChain, LlamaIndex, or AutoGen); the domain-specific tools the agent calls are a build decision (proprietary APIs into the firm's systems); and novel agent capabilities (multi-agent coordination patterns not available in commercial frameworks) may be a partner decision with a research institution or AI lab.

attrs.alt: Build-Buy-Partner decision matrix for AI capabilities

content.text: Each column shows a decision path with its indicators, cost profile, and risk type. The decision is not sequential — evaluate all three options simultaneously using the four dimensions. The right column for each capability depends on the combination of strategic differentiation, build feasibility, market maturity, and data sovereignty requirements.

Apply the build-buy-partner framework for every capability in the portfolio before committing to a delivery approach. For new capabilities proposed mid-program, apply the framework before adding the capability to the delivery queue. Re-evaluate all buy decisions annually and all partner decisions at contract renewal — the technology market changes fast enough that a buy decision from 18 months ago may be suboptimal today. Re-evaluate build decisions when the team experiences significant delivery delays, which often indicates the capability should have been a buy.

Decompose capabilities: for each use case, list the distinct AI capabilities it requires (e.g., document extraction, classification, generation, orchestration, tool use). Apply the matrix independently to each capability, not to the use case as a whole.

Score on the four dimensions: for each capability, score strategic differentiation (high/medium/low), build feasibility (high/medium/low), market maturity (mature/emerging/nascent), and data sovereignty (controlled environment required: yes/no).

Apply the decision rules: a capability with high differentiation + high build feasibility = build; low differentiation + mature market + no sovereignty constraint = buy; high differentiation + low build feasibility = partner.
Document the drivers: record the specific reasons for each decision. When the decision is revisited later, the recorded drivers allow an evidence-based comparison with the current situation.

Run vendor evaluation in parallel with build feasibility assessment: it is common to discover during vendor evaluation that the market is more or less mature than assumed, which changes the build/buy calculus.

Get CISO review for buy decisions: vendor-provided AI capabilities involve data leaving the firm's network; security review of the vendor's data handling and retention policies is required before finalizing a buy decision.

# Build-Buy-Partner scoring model
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class CapabilityAssessment:
    name: str
    strategic_differentiation: str   # 'high' | 'medium' | 'low'
    build_feasibility: str           # 'high' | 'medium' | 'low'
    market_maturity: str             # 'mature' | 'emerging' | 'nascent'
    data_sovereignty_required: bool
    estimated_build_months: int
    vendor_solution_exists: bool
    vendor_quality_adequate: bool

def recommend(ca: CapabilityAssessment) -> str:
    # Sovereignty override: must stay in controlled env
    if ca.data_sovereignty_required and not ca.vendor_solution_exists:
        return "BUILD (sovereignty required)"

    # Commodity: low differentiation + mature market + vendor exists
    if (ca.strategic_differentiation == "low" and
        ca.market_maturity == "mature" and
        ca.vendor_solution_exists and ca.vendor_quality_adequate):
        return "BUY"

    # High value but cannot build: partner
    if (ca.strategic_differentiation == "high" and
        ca.build_feasibility == "low"):
        return "PARTNER"

    # High value and can build
    if (ca.strategic_differentiation == "high" and
        ca.build_feasibility in ("high", "medium")):
        return "BUILD"

    # Default: buy if vendor exists and quality is adequate
    if ca.vendor_solution_exists and ca.vendor_quality_adequate:
        return "BUY"
    return "PARTNER"

capabilities = [
    CapabilityAssessment("Document OCR extraction", "low", "high", "mature", False, 6, True, True),
    CapabilityAssessment("Proprietary risk scoring model", "high", "high", "nascent", True, 12, False, False),
    CapabilityAssessment("Agent orchestration framework", "low", "medium", "emerging", False, 8, True, True),
    CapabilityAssessment("Novel multi-agent research", "high", "low", "nascent", False, 24, False, False),
]
for cap in capabilities:
    print(f"{cap.name:<40} -> {recommend(cap)}")

# Vendor dependency risk assessment for buy decisions
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class VendorRisk:
    vendor: str
    capability: str
    annual_spend_usd: float
    switching_cost_months: int  # months to migrate to alternative
    alternative_vendors: int
    data_egress: bool           # does data leave firm boundary?
    sla_uptime: float           # e.g. 0.999

def risk_score(vr: VendorRisk) -> dict:
    concentration = "HIGH" if vr.alternative_vendors < 2 else "MEDIUM" if vr.alternative_vendors < 4 else "LOW"
    lock_in = "HIGH" if vr.switching_cost_months > 12 else "MEDIUM" if vr.switching_cost_months > 6 else "LOW"
    data_risk = "HIGH" if vr.data_egress else "LOW"
    financial_exposure = "HIGH" if vr.annual_spend_usd > 1_000_000 else "MEDIUM" if vr.annual_spend_usd > 200_000 else "LOW"
    mitigations = []
    if concentration == "HIGH":
        mitigations.append("Negotiate multi-vendor contract escape clause")
    if lock_in == "HIGH":
        mitigations.append("Maintain internal capability prototype to enable migration")
    if data_risk == "HIGH":
        mitigations.append("Enforce data processing agreement; require SOC2 Type II")
    return {
        "vendor": vr.vendor,
        "concentration_risk": concentration,
        "lock_in_risk": lock_in,
        "data_risk": data_risk,
        "financial_exposure": financial_exposure,
        "mitigations": mitigations
    }

vendors = [
    VendorRisk("OpenAI", "LLM API", 600_000, 9, 3, True, 0.999),
    VendorRisk("Proprietary OCR vendor", "Document extraction", 250_000, 18, 1, True, 0.998),
]
for v in vendors:
    risk = risk_score(v)
    print(f"{risk['vendor']}: concentration={risk['concentration_risk']}, lock-in={risk['lock_in_risk']}")
    for m in risk['mitigations']:
        print(f"  Mitigation: {m}")

A UK-based asset manager evaluated its AI capability requirements across three use cases: research summarization, portfolio risk analysis, and client reporting. For research summarization, the team scored: strategic differentiation = low (competitors could access the same vendor), market maturity = mature (several capable LLM APIs existed), build feasibility = medium, data sovereignty = no. The decision was BUY — they selected a leading LLM API with a data processing agreement that met their regulatory requirements.

For portfolio risk analysis, the scores were: strategic differentiation = high (the firm's proprietary risk factor model was a competitive differentiator), build feasibility = high (the quant team had the ML expertise), data sovereignty = yes (position data cannot leave the firm's controlled environment). The decision was BUILD — they fine-tuned an open-weights model on proprietary data and deployed it on-premise. For client reporting, the decision was PARTNER with a fintech company that had distribution relationships the asset manager lacked; the IP agreement granted the asset manager exclusive rights to the reporting module for their client segment.
Applying the decision at use case level rather than capability level: a single use case may have three capabilities that belong in three different decision columns.

Not including CISO in buy decisions: vendor AI solutions involve data handling arrangements that security teams must review; a buy decision made without security review often gets blocked at deployment.

Underestimating build cost by excluding maintenance: the decision model should compare 5-year total cost of ownership, including model retraining, infrastructure, and engineering overhead, not just initial build cost.
Treating the decision as permanent: revisit build decisions annually and buy decisions at contract renewal — the technology market changes fast enough to shift the optimal decision.

Using partner decisions to avoid hard build/buy choices: partnership is appropriate when the firm genuinely needs an external organization's capability; it is not appropriate as a way to defer a funding decision.
Decompose every use case into capabilities before applying the matrix: the decision is always at capability level, never at use case level.
Build an internal prototype even for buy decisions: a time-boxed 2-week prototype validates vendor claims and preserves the option to switch if the vendor relationship deteriorates.

Quantify switching cost explicitly for every buy decision: 'what would it take to migrate away from this vendor in 12 months?' — this single question surfaces lock-in risk before the contract is signed.

Document build decisions with a re-evaluation trigger: specify the conditions under which the build decision will be re-evaluated (e.g., 'if a vendor achieves SOC2 Type II certification with on-premise deployment').
Negotiate data handling terms before finalizing buy decisions: a vendor that cannot agree to acceptable data processing terms is not a viable buy candidate regardless of capability quality.

The build-buy-partner matrix scales as a quarterly review process: the full portfolio of capabilities is reviewed once per year; individual capabilities with significant market changes or delivery problems are reviewed quarterly. The KPI for matrix quality is the ratio of capabilities in the right quadrant at the 18-month mark — what percentage of build decisions delivered on the strategic differentiation promise, and what percentage of buy decisions are delivering acceptable quality without unacceptable vendor dependency?
Template per capability: drivers, scores, decision, review date
Standard questionnaire: data handling, SLAs, SOC2, exit terms
Define IP ownership, exclusivity, exit provisions upfront
Capability, decision, vendor (if buy), review date, owner
5-year TCO for build vs buy comparison

Security review is mandatory before finalizing any buy decision. Key questions: Does the vendor have SOC2 Type II certification? What data does the vendor retain after inference — zero retention is required for sensitive data? Is on-premise or VPC deployment available for data sovereignty use cases? What are the vendor's incident notification SLAs? A vendor that cannot answer these questions satisfactorily is not a viable buy candidate for enterprise AI workloads. For build decisions, security must be designed in from the start — retrofitting access controls, audit logging, and encryption after initial deployment is significantly more expensive.
Apply the build-buy-partner decision at capability level, not use case level — a single use case may span all three columns.
The four decision drivers are: strategic differentiation, build feasibility, market maturity, and data sovereignty — evaluate all four before reaching a conclusion.
Buy decisions require explicit vendor dependency risk assessment, including switching cost and data handling terms, before the contract is signed.
The decision is not permanent — revisit build decisions annually and buy decisions at contract renewal as the technology market evolves.
Partner decisions require upfront IP agreement — who owns what the partner relationship creates, and under what conditions can the firm use it independently.
CISO review is mandatory for all buy decisions — vendor AI solutions involve data handling arrangements that cannot be evaluated without security expertise.
